title: Complex numbers
summary: 9709 P3 complex numbers — Argand diagrams, modulus, argument, polar form, and De Moivre's theorem with GeoGebra complex plane explorer.
learningObjectives: Perform arithmetic with complex numbers in Cartesian form.
Plot numbers on an Argand diagram and find modulus and argument.
Convert between Cartesian and polar/exponential forms.
Apply De Moivre's theorem to powers and roots of complex numbers.

simpleExplanation.title: Numbers with imaginary parts
simpleExplanation.summary: Complex numbers extend the real line to a plane. z = x + iy maps to (x, y); polar form uses distance r and angle θ from the positive real axis.
steps: Add/subtract component-wise; multiply using (a+bi)(c+di) = ac − bd + i(ad + bc).
Modulus |z| = √(x² + y²); argument arg z measured anticlockwise from positive real axis.
Polar: z = r(cos θ + i sin θ) = re^(iθ).
De Moivre: (cos θ + i sin θ)ⁿ = cos nθ + i sin nθ — use for zⁿ and nth roots.

flashcards.back: a − bi — product with z gives |z|² (real).
(cos θ + i sin θ)ⁿ = cos nθ + i sin nθ

sections.content: **Complex numbers** are a core P3 topic — loci in the Argand diagram, solving polynomial equations with non-real roots, and De Moivre appear regularly on Paper 3.
|z| = √(x² + y²) | arg z = tan⁻¹(y/x) (correct quadrant) | z* = x − iy | z z* = |z|²

sections.question: Express (1 + i)⁶ in the form x + iy.

sections.content: For loci questions, sketch first: **|z − a| = r** is a circle; **|z − a| = |z − b|** is a perpendicular bisector; **arg(z − a) = α** is a half-line.
